q: Everything around us is made up of elements, molecules, or ___.
The attractive forces that hold atoms together in compounds are also known as ___.
The outermost shell electrons (___) of those atoms are the basis for understanding the joining of atoms.

In order to explain the electronic structures of atoms, the principles of filling electrons in the atomic orbitals, such as the ___, Pauli’s exclusion principle, and Hund’s rule are fundamental to understand.
Each subshell can be filled with 2, 6, 10, and 14 electrons, ___.
$s$ subshell has only one ___ orbital.
There are three dumbbell-shaped $p$ orbitals and the five $d$ orbitals have ___ shapes.
According to the Aufbau principle (Aufbau means ___ in German), electrons are filled in the lower energy atomic orbitals before filling higher energy ones.
One of the properties of the electron, known as ___, has to be considered.
Each electron spins on an axis, like a ___.
Pauli’s exclusion principle states that no more than two electrons can occupy the same orbital, and two electrons in the same orbital must have ___ spins.
Hund’s rule states that every orbital in a subshell is ___ occupied with one electron before any one orbital is doubly occupied with opposite spins.
The electrons in the valence shells (outermost shells) of an atom are known as the ___.
For an atom, the number of valence electrons is equal to its ___ in the Periodic Table.
Whenever a chemical bond forms, atoms lose, gain, or share electrons in order to achieve the stable electronic structures of ___.
Eight electrons in the valence shell is known as the ___.
The chemically important valence electrons occupied in $s$ and $p$ orbitals are represented using ___ introduced by Gilbert Newton Lewis.
The strength of sharing or transferring the valence electrons depends on the ___ of the atoms involved in the chemical bonding.
Since electronegativity (EN) is a measure of the ability of an atom to attract the ___ to itself, the greater an element’s electronegativity, the stronger its attractive power to electrons will be.
___ is the amount of energy released when an electron is added to a gaseous atom.
___ is the amount of energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom.
The elements having high electron affinity easily gain electrons, resulting in the formation of the ___.
The elements having low ionisation energy easily lose electrons, resulting in the formation of the ___.
The type of chemical bonding depends on the types of elements (___) that are involved.
Ionic bonding (electrovalent bonding) results from the ___ from one atom having small ionisation energy to another atom having a large electron affinity.
Ionic bonding depends on the difference in ___ between two atoms.
Mostly it takes place when the difference in electronegativity is greater than ___.
When an ionic bond forms, a metal loses electrons to form positively charged ions (___).
The non-metal atom readily gains electrons to form negatively charged ions (___).
The ratio in which positive and negative ions combine is the ratio that achieves ___ of the resulting compound.
In general, the greater the charge on the ions, the greater the electrostatic attraction and the ___ the ionic bond will be.
In solid ionic compounds, their ions are held in ___ and cannot move, and hence they cannot conduct electricity.
The crystalline structure of an ionic solid is called a ___.
The smallest repeating unit of a crystal lattice that shows the three-dimensional pattern of the entire crystal is called a ___.
The cubic unit cell can have three types: simple cubic ($sc$) unit cell, face-centred cubic ($fcc$) unit cell, and ___ ($bcc$) unit cell.
The term ___ is used to express the number of ions surrounding the central ion in the lattice.
Covalent bonding can occur between similar or even ___ atoms.
The concept of ___ rather than electron transfer explains bonding between similar or identical atoms.
A covalent bond is the electrostatic attraction between the ___ and the shared electrons.
Covalent bonding usually occurs between ___.
In covalent bonding, the outermost orbitals of the atoms overlap so that ___ in each of the bonding atoms can be shared.
___ in each of the bonding atoms are not involved in bonding.
In single covalent bonds, only ___ of electrons is shared between two atoms.
A double bond is formed by sharing ___ of electrons between two atoms.
A triple bond is formed by sharing ___ of electrons.
The higher the number of bonded electrons, the stronger the attraction between the two atoms and the ___ the bond length.
A ___, or dative bond, is a special type of covalent bond.
A coordinate bond is characterised by the fact that the two shared electrons are both ___ of the participating atoms.
A coordinate bond is expressed as an ___ which indicates the direction in which the electrons are donated.
Molecules with incomplete octets are called ___.
Lewis structures are only ___ representations of how the valence shell electrons are arranged around individual atoms in a molecule or an ion.
Molecules that deviate from the octet rule, such as electron-deficient molecules and ___, can occur.
An expanded valence shell molecule has valence electrons of more than ___.
The distribution of electronic structure of a molecule can be explained using the ___ (MOT).
VSEPR stands for ___ theory.
This model assumes that the electron pairs will arrange themselves to ___ from one another.
These pairs of electrons may be expressed as ___ and/or bonding pairs that can influence the molecular shape.
The lone pairs are localised on the central atom while each ___ is shared between two atoms.
The repulsion between lone pairs of electrons is ___ as compared to the lone pair-bonding pair and bonding pair-bonding pair repulsions.
Repulsion effects result in deviations from the idealised shapes and alternations in ___ in molecules.
All ___ molecules represent two points in space and are joined by a straight line.
A triatomic molecule ($AX_2$ type) has a ___.
Some elements occur in solids where the atoms are held together by single covalent bonds, existing as ___.
In a diamond, each carbon atom is joined to ___ other carbon atoms by strong covalent bonds.
In diamond, the carbon atoms form a regular ___ network resulting in a giant structure.
In the case of graphite, each carbon atom is joined to the other ___ carbon atoms by covalent bonds.
Each layer in graphite is formed with ___ of carbon atoms.
There are weak ___ forces between the layers of carbon atoms in graphite.
Graphite can conduct electricity because it has ___ in each layer.
Fullerene ($C_{60}$), carbon nanotubes, and graphene do not exist as giant structures but are only ___.
Silicon(IV) oxide, $SiO_2$ (silica or quartz) is an example of a ___ molecule.
In quartz, each oxygen atom is bonded to only ___ silicon atoms.
Quartz does not conduct electricity because it has no ___.
Bond angle is an angle between any ___ that include a common atom, usually measured in degrees.
The attractive and repulsive forces that arise between molecules are known as ___.
Intermolecular forces are responsible for most of the ___ properties of matter.
If the atoms in a molecule are not identical, the electrons will ___.
Bond polarity within a molecule results in a ___ over the molecule.
A ___ of the molecule can be expressed numerically by taking into account the magnitude of the charge and the distance separating the charges.
The electronegativity of bonded atoms and the ___ of molecules play an important role in determining their polarity.
If two different non-metal atoms are bonded, then the atom with the ___ will attract the shared electron pair more strongly.
A polar covalent bond results where one atom will have a partially negative charge ($\delta^-$), and the other will have a partially positive charge ($\delta^+$), known as a ___.
The bond becomes ___ as the difference in electronegativity values of the atoms in a covalent bond increases.
In a covalent bond formed between two similar non-metal atoms, the shared electron pair is equally attracted to the two atoms resulting in a ___.
The direction of the dipole can be shown by the ___ sign.
The direction of the arrow points to the ___ of the dipole.
All ___ molecules containing polar covalent bonds are polar molecules since they have dipole moments.
In the case of polyatomic molecules, their dipole moments depend not only on the individual bond dipoles but also on the ___ of the molecules.
The dipole moment of a molecule is the ___ of the dipole moments of various bonds.
Intermolecular forces can be classified as ___ and hydrogen bonding.
van der Waals forces are weak forces which include dipole-dipole interaction, ion-dipole interaction, and ___.
Dipole-dipole interactions are formed when two ___ interact with each other in space.
___ is formed from the interaction between an ion and a dipole molecule.
The weakest intermolecular forces that can be formed between atoms, as well as non-polar molecules, are known as ___.
London dispersion forces are named after the German physicist ___.
Because of the constant motion of the electrons, an atom can develop a ___ when the electrons are distributed unsymmetrically about the nucleus.
London dispersion force is also called ___ interaction.
London dispersion forces are instantaneous dipole-dipole interactions that exist between all atoms and molecules, ___.
The formation of hydrogen bonds between covalent molecules requires the first molecule to have hydrogen attached to a ___ (F, O, or N).
The formation of hydrogen bonds between covalent molecules requires the second molecule to possess a ___ on a small electronegative atom (F, O, or N).
A hydrogen bond is a special type of ___ interaction that occurs between polar molecules.
The hydrogen bond is ___ than a covalent bond and is the strongest of all intermolecular forces.
Most of the metals are solids at room temperature and are held together in solid crystalline form by a ___.
In a metallic bond, the atoms do not ___ to bond together.
In metallic solids, the valence electrons of the atoms are very loosely held, forming a ___.
Mobile electrons are often described as a ___ which make the metals easily conduct electricity.
Metals consist of giant structures of atoms that are packed closely together in a regular arrangement called a ___.
The strength of metallic bonding increases with the increasing ___ on the ions in the metal lattice.
The strength of metallic bonding increases with the ___ of metal ions in the lattice.
The strength of metallic bonding increases with the increasing number of ___ (valence electrons) per atom.
The strength of metallic bond is lower than that of an ___.
